Title: Machine learning in microseismic monitoring
Application: Survey of ML methods for microseismic detection, phase picking, classification (event vs noise), localization, magnitude estimation, and source mechanism/moment tensor analysis; discussion of real-time monitoring and operational workflows
Country: Helmholtz Centre Potsdam – GFZ (Germany); King Abdullah University of Science and Technology – KAUST (Saudi Arabia); King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals – KFUPM (Saudi Arabia); Tsinghua University (China); Delft University of Technology – TU Delft (Netherlands); Seismik s.r.o. (Czech Republic)
University/Institute: Provide a comprehensive review and synthesis of ML techniques applied to microseismic monitoring; outline tasks, datasets, common pipelines, challenges (imbalance, labeling, domain shift), and future directions
Purpose / Objective: Microseismic waveform time series (1C/3C geophones, DAS), event catalogs/metadata, synthetic waveforms; occasionally spectrogram representations
Data Type: Varies widely across studies; often limited labeled events with large volumes of noise/background
Training Data Size: Fixed-length waveform windows per channel (samples × channels); or 2D time–frequency images (spectrograms); sometimes multi-station tensors
Input Shape: Mix of hand-crafted features (e.g., STA/LTA, spectral moments, polarization, envelopes) and learned features via deep nets (CNN feature maps)
Number of Classes / Labels: Microseismic events induced by hydraulic fracturing, geothermal stimulation, CO₂ sequestration, mining, reservoir operations; plus non-event noise classes
Event Types: Human analyst picks and catalogs; semi-automatic pickers curated by experts; synthetic labels from physics-based simulation
Label Source: Study-dependent; common splits are station-wise or time-wise; cross-validation within site; some use hold-out wells or stages; limited standardized benchmarks

Title: Microseism Detection Method in Coal Mine Based on Spatiotemporal Characteristics and Support Vector Regression Algorithm
Category: Microseismic monitoring; Mining safety; Signal processing
Application: Real‑time detection of coal‑mine microseisms and anomaly/energy estimation in working faces
Type of Paper: Peer‑reviewed journal article (Applied Sciences, MDPI); Open Access (CC BY 4.0)
University/Institute: Anhui University of Science & Technology; China Coal Technology & Engineering Group Shenyang Research Institute; State Key Laboratory of Coal Mine Safety Technology; Henan Polytechnic University
Purpose / Objective: Improve sensitivity and accuracy of coal‑mine microseism detection by extracting spatiotemporal features (Markov‑chain based) and feeding them to an SVR model whose hyperparameters are optimized via an improved particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm; deliver accurate, real‑time detection and reduce missed detections and lag
Data Type: Coal‑mine vibration/microseismic sensor signals (time series) collected online; distributed across 9 operation points; includes controlled tapping and a microseismic simulation accident
Training Data Size: Not reported (NR) explicitly; experiments include six timestamped events around the accident and 5 taps × 9 points for stability tests
Input Shape: Spatiotemporal sliding window over node time series; features formed from 9×9 temporal state‑transition probability matrix and 9×9 cross‑state (spatial) transition probability matrix (states a–i)
Event Types: Pipeline tapping, bolt tapping, wall tapping, shouting; microseismic accident simulation
Label Source: “Actual energy (J)” records for events; comparative metrics (missed detection, misjudgment) computed from detection outcomes

Title: Machine learning-assisted processing workflow for multi-fiber DAS microseismic data
Category: Seismology · Geophysical Signal Processing · Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS)
Application: Microseismic monitoring during hydraulic fracturing (multi-well, multi-fiber DAS)
University/Institute: University of Calgary (Dept. of Geoscience); The University of Texas at Austin (Bureau of Economic Geology)
Purpose / Objective: Develop an ML-assisted workflow for DAS microseismic processing: CNN for event detection and U‑Net for P/S arrival picking; demonstrate on two field datasets and compare to co‑located 3C geophones; enable near‑real‑time monitoring post‑training.
Data Type: DAS strain / strain‑rate time series (multi‑fiber, horizontal + vertical wells); co‑located 3‑component borehole geophones for comparison
Training Data Size: CNN detection: 3,584 2D samples (1,792 event, 1,792 noise); U‑Net picking: ~4,000 labeled 1D traces (Stage 21 common events)
Input Shape: CNN: 512×512 (space–time windows); U‑Net: 3001×1 samples (~1.5 s) per trace
Number of Classes / Labels: CNN detection: 2 (event, noise); U‑Net picking: 3 (P, S, noise)
Event Types: Induced microseismic events during hydraulic fracturing; includes distant/off‑well events
Label Source: Preliminary STA/LTA detections + manual picks for events and P/S arrivals

Title: A Survey of Machine Learning Applications in Microseismic Signal Recognition and Classification
Application: Recognition & classification of microseismic signals in mining safety/monitoring
University/Institute: International College of Digital Innovation, Chiang Mai University; Office of International Collaboration and Exchange, Chengdu University
Purpose / Objective: Review recent machine learning applications for microseismic signal recognition and classification; compare traditional vs ML methods; categorize supervised/unsupervised approaches; summarize advances; outline challenges and future directions.
Data Type: N/A (survey)
Training Data Size: N/A (survey)
Input Shape: N/A (survey)
Event Types: Microseismic events, blasting, drilling, noise (various operational equipment)
Label Source: N/A (survey)

Title: RESEARCH ON MICROSEISMIC EVENT LOCALIZATION BASED ON CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK
Application: Hydraulic fracturing microseismic source localization (event positioning)
Type of Paper: Journal research article (Journal of Seismic Exploration)
University/Institute: a) School of Computer and Information Technology, Northeast Petroleum University, Daqing, Heilongjiang, China; b) School of Medical Information Engineering, Heilongjiang University of Chinese Medicine, Harbin, Heilongjiang, China; c) Artificial Intelligence Energy Research Institute, Northeast Petroleum University, Daqing, Heilongjiang, China; d) Key Laboratory of Networking and Intelligent Control of Heilongjiang Province, Daqing, Heilongjiang, China
Purpose / Objective: Propose MEL-Net (a CNN based on U‑Net with attention and atrous spatial pyramid pooling) to directly map microseismic waveforms to a 2D probability map of source location, improving accuracy, robustness to noise/velocity-model errors, and speed vs. reverse-time methods.
Data Type: Synthetic 2D microseismic records (simple layered model and complex Marmousi model); single- and multi-source scenarios; additive Gaussian noise for robustness tests; velocity-model smoothing to simulate model errors.
Training Data Size: Large-scale synthetic dataset constructed via forward modeling; exact count not reported. Example test sets: 120 samples (simple model) and 65 samples (Marmousi).
Input Shape: 2D gathers with time resampled to match depth grid (Nz). Simple model grid: 256×200 (5 m spacing); Marmousi: 288×300 (8 m spacing). Sampling: 0.25 ms (simple), 0.3 ms (Marmousi); record lengths 1800 ms and 2700 ms respectively.
Number of Classes / Labels: Not a classification task; supervised regression to a probability image.
Event Types: Single-source and multi-source (3–4 sources to mimic slender fractures).
Label Source: 2D Gaussian label (peak=1) centered at true source coordinates; probability map same size as monitoring grid.

Title: Microseismic event detection based on multiscale detection convolutional neural network
Category: Microseismic event detection; signal processing; deep learning
Application: Hydraulic fracturing microseismic monitoring; event detection in seismic traces (synthetic + field)
Type of Paper: Journal research article (Journal of Seismic Exploration, 32:455–477)
University/Institute: Northeast Petroleum University (School of Physics and Electronic Engineering; AI Energy Research Institute; Key Laboratory of Networking & Intelligent Control of Heilongjiang Province)
Purpose / Objective: Propose MSD-CNN with multiscale detectors and a Certainty Factor (C‑F) uncertainty reasoning model to improve microseismic event detection accuracy and noise robustness without deepening the network.
Data Type: Synthetic 1‑D traces (Ricker-convolved, varying SNR) and field data from four fractured wells
Training Data Size: Training set: 4,000 samples; Validation used during training; Test set examples shown: 800 samples (balanced)
Input Shape: 1‑D single-trace windows; sliding window length 100 samples; step 20
Label Source: One‑hot labels from preprocessing: positive windows containing events labeled 1; noise 0

Title: A Deep Learning Approach for Microseismic Detection with Downhole DAS
Application: Detection & location of microseismic events during CO₂ injection at the Otway test facility (downhole DAS)
Type of Paper: Conference/Workshop extended abstract (4th EAGE Workshop on Fiber Optic Sensing for Energy Applications)
University/Institute: Curtin University (MinEx CRC acknowledged; CO₂CRC data access)
Purpose / Objective: Train a supervised DL model on (semi-)synthetic DAS data to (i) detect microseismic events and (ii) estimate event depth (z), offset (x), and subsurface properties Vp, Vs, and density ρ; then apply to field DAS data from Otway
Data Type: Distributed Acoustic Sensing (downhole), passive microseismic; synthetic + recorded field noise; field application dataset from Otway
Training Data Size: 5,365 semi-synthetic event patches + 1,073 pure-noise patches (labels zero)
Input Shape: 512×512 grayscale image patches (1 channel) derived from DAS data (3‑s windows)
Number of Classes / Labels: Regression targets: event detection score + location (z, x) and subsurface properties (Vp, Vs, ρ); noise patches labeled zero
Event Types: Passive microseismic events triggered during CO₂ injection
Label Source: Synthetic modeling for labels (z, x, Vp, Vs, ρ); manual picks/locations used for additional validation on field data

Title: Graph Neural Networks for Microseismic Event Detection: Focusing on Distributed Acoustic Sensing Data
Category: Microseismic event detection; Graph Neural Networks; Distributed Acoustic Sensing
Application: Detect microseismic events from DAS recordings
University/Institute: Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS; Universitas Islam Indonesia; University of Ruse “Angel Kanchev”
Purpose / Objective: Propose and evaluate GNNs (GCN/GAT) to model spatiotemporal relationships in DAS for improved microseismic event detection
Data Type: DAS passive VSP and continuous Silixa iDAS Carina recordings
Training Data Size: ~13 TB; 11 days; 1280 channels; 2,000 Hz sampling; ~425 events labeled
Input Shape: Fixed-length time windows (few seconds to minutes) across 1280 channels @ 2 kHz; exact window length not specified
Number of Classes / Labels: Binary (event vs. noise) — implied (not explicitly enumerated)
Label Source: Manually labeled by experts

Title: Spiking Neural Network for Microseismic Events Detection Using Distributed Acoustic Sensing Data
Category: Microseismic event detection; Geophysics; Signal Processing
Application: Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) microseismic monitoring
Type of Paper: Conference paper (SCDM 2024), Lecture Notes in Networks and Systems (LNNS) vol. 1078
University/Institute: Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS (UTP)
Purpose / Objective: Propose a spiking neural network (SNN) approach to improve precision, reduce overfitting, and enhance interpretability for DAS-based microseismic event detection; outline a three-phase methodology and suitability for real-time DAS data management
Data Type: DAS microseismic data (general)
Training Data Size: Not reported
Input Shape: Not reported
Label Source: Not reported

Title: Automatic arrival‑time picking of P‑ and S‑waves of micro‑seismic events based on relative standard generative adversarial network and GHRA
Category: Exploration Engineering; Micro‑seismic signal processing; Arrival‑time picking
Application: Automatic, fast and accurate picking of micro‑seismic P/S arrival times for monitoring/early‑warning; deployed on low‑power devices
University/Institute: Hebei Key Laboratory of Seismic Disaster Instrument and Monitoring Technology; Institute of Disaster Prevention, Langfang, China
Purpose / Objective: Propose LAU‑Net, a lightweight adversarial U‑shaped network with GHRA layer and an application‑aware loss to improve precision/speed of P/S arrival‑time picking and enable on‑device deployment
Data Type: 1D three‑component ground micro‑seismic waveforms (Shengli Oilfield, Sichuan) with expert confidence labels
Training Data Size: 8,986 events (train/val); 473 events (held‑out test); fivefold cross‑validation also reported
Input Shape: 1D segment length 1600 samples (per Table of pickup network)
Number of Classes / Labels: Two probability curves (P‑arrival, S‑arrival) over time samples (point/sequence labeling)
Event Types: Micro‑seismic events from oilfield monitoring (2011–2017; additional 2019–2020 generalization set)
Label Source: Multiple micro‑seismic experts; merged and converted to confidence‑probability labels

Title: Transfer Learning-Based Seismic Phase Detection Algorithm for Distributed Acoustic Sensing Microseismic Data
Application: DAS-based microseismic monitoring (Utah FORGE geothermal / CCS monitoring context)
Type of Paper: Research article (IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing)
Country: South Korea (authors); field data from USA (Utah FORGE)
University/Institute: Hanyang University, Department of Earth Resources and Environmental Engineering
Purpose / Objective: Develop a transfer learning (TL)–based algorithm that adapts a generalized earthquake phase detector to DAS microseismic data, enabling robust P- and S-phase detection with very limited labeled DAS events.
Data Type: 3‑component earthquake seismograms (training); single‑component DAS microseismic traces (target); additional K‑NET/KiK‑NET earthquake records for generalization tests.
Training Data Size: ~1,000,000 earthquake samples from STEAD (pretraining).
Input Shape: 60 s windows at 100 Hz (≈ 6000 samples) with 3 channels for pretraining; during TL, single‑channel DAS + two zero channels to emulate 3‑component input.
Number of Classes / Labels: 3 probabilistic outputs: event detection, P‑arrival, S‑arrival (sequence labeling over time).
Event Types: Global earthquakes (training/validation) and injection‑induced microseismicity (Utah FORGE)
Label Source: STEAD catalog P/S picks (training); two manually picked DAS events for TL; prior P‑arrival picks for K‑NET/KiK‑NET used for evaluation.

Title: A comparison of deep and shallow models for the detection of induced seismicity
Application: Detection of low‑magnitude induced earthquakes in the Groningen Gas Field (Netherlands)
Country: Dataset: Netherlands (Groningen Gas Field); Authors’ affiliations: UK (UCL), India (Rocket Learning – Ekho Foundation)
University/Institute: Department of Computer Science, University College London (UCL), UK; Rocket Learning – Ekho Foundation, Delhi, India
Purpose / Objective: Compare an interpretable logistic regression (LR) model (with engineered statistical features) against a deep convolutional neural network (CNN) for induced seismicity detection; evaluate performance across increasing class‑imbalance (IR) levels and analyze false positives/negatives.
Data Type: Seismic waveform time series from KNMI G‑network (4 geophone levels × 3 components)
Training Data Size: Positives: 2,300 event‑labelled seismograms; Negatives: 4,000 noise seismograms for baseline IR; additional 38,100 noise samples used to raise IR in test sets
Input Shape: For CNN: (4 geophones, 3001 time points, 3 channels) i.e., (4, 3001, 3)
Label Source: KNMI metadata (event picks, station info)

Title: Optimized Microseismic Event Detection and Source Analysis at the Quest CCS Site, Alberta, Canada
Category: Microseismic monitoring; CCS; Seismology; Signal processing
Application: Event detection and source analysis to support seismic‑hazard management and reservoir characterization for CO₂ storage
Country: Canada (study site in Alberta); multinational author affiliations
University/Institute: NORSAR; University of Alberta; Shell Canada; GFZ German Research Centre for Geosciences; Shell Global Solutions International B.V.; TotalEnergies; CVA for TotalEnergies; Alcatel Submarine Networks
Purpose / Objective: Compare detection performance of downhole geophones, downhole DAS, and surface nodes; apply advanced processing (CATS, template matching, PhaseNet) to lower detection thresholds; perform relative relocation (modified double‑difference) to refine spatial clustering
Data Type: Continuous passive seismic recordings: 8‑level downhole geophones (DMW); downhole DAS fiber in central injection well; 153 surface nodes in 17 sub‑arrays; vendor STA/LTA event catalogs
Training Data Size: Not applicable (unsupervised/thresholding + physics‑based); template libraries built from 5 years of cataloged events (2017–2021) grouped into 32 libraries
Input Shape: Time‑series waveforms; time–frequency spectrograms (CATS); array beams/semblance stacks; cross‑correlation traces
Number of Classes / Labels: Not applicable (detection task). Ancillary categorization of non‑seismic vs microseismic signals
Event Types: Microseismic events (Precambrian basement); anthropogenic signals (vibroseis sweeps, mine blasts, train noise, tube waves)
Label Source: Downhole geophone STA/LTA reference catalog (vendor); additional AOR events from CATS and template‑matching detections

Title: How induced earthquakes respond to pre‑existing fractures and hydraulic fracturing operations? A case study in South China
Category: Induced Seismicity • Hydraulic Fracturing • Earthquake Seismology
Application: Shale gas production; seismic hazard understanding/mitigation in Cen’gong block (South China)
Country: China (study area: Cen’gong shale gas block, Tongren, Guizhou); Canada & China (affiliations)
University/Institute: Zhejiang University; China University of Mining and Technology (Beijing); Dalhousie University
Purpose / Objective: Build a high‑precision catalog and focal mechanisms for HF‑induced earthquakes; quantify roles of pre‑existing fractures vs. stimulation operations; analyze distribution, magnitudes, trailing seismicity, directional migration; provide mitigation implications
Data Type: 3D seismic & Ant Tracking attribute; geological/structural context; well logs & core data; HF injection pressure/rate & stage timelines; dense temporary seismic array (41 3‑component geophones, 500 Hz); microseismic records
Training Data Size: Pretrained ML models used (PhaseNet, DiTingMotion); no model training on study data
Input Shape: Seismic waveforms sampled at 500 Hz; P/S phases picked; events with ≥20 P arrivals used for focal mechanism solutions
Number of Classes / Labels: First‑motion polarity classes: Up / Down / Uncertain (for DiTingMotion); focal mechanism types dominated by strike‑slip
Label Source: First‑motion polarities via DiTingMotion; focal mechanisms via HASH (using polarities + P/S ratios)

Title: MA_W‑Net-Based Dual-Output Method for Microseismic Localization in Strong Noise Environments
Category: Microseismic localization; Deep learning; Attention mechanisms; Denoising
Application: Localizing microseismic sources and denoising seismic records under low SNR (−5, −10, −15 dB)
University/Institute: College of Geoexploration Science and Technology, Jilin University
Purpose / Objective: Propose MA_W‑Net (U‑Net with dual decoders + attention) to improve microseismic localization accuracy and noise suppression at very low SNRs
Data Type: Synthetic microseismic records generated via finite-difference simulation on Marmousi model; grayscale images
Training Data Size: 4000 samples (train/test split 9:1)
Input Shape: 512 × 64 × 1 (time × channels × depth as grayscale map)
Number of Classes / Labels: Not a classification task; continuous heatmap (Gaussian mixture) indicating source locations
Label Source: Synthetic labels from known source locations, represented as Gaussian mixture maps (normalized to [0,1])

Title: Supervised microseismic event detection using Siamese Networks for labelling of noisy recordings
Category: Seismic signal processing; microseismic event detection & classification
Application: Automated labelling of microseismic recordings (earthquakes, micro‑quakes, rockfalls, anthropogenic noise) from landslide monitoring (Super‑Sauze, France)
Type of Paper: Research preprint (supervised classification; Siamese CNN); not peer‑reviewed yet
DOI: No formal DOI; SSRN abstract ID 4762424
University/Institute: University of Strathclyde, Dept. of Electronic & Electrical Engineering (Glasgow, UK)
Purpose / Objective: Propose a supervised Siamese network and data‑driven anchor selection to reduce expert effort for labelling noisy microseismic recordings and classify events into four types.
Data Type: Seismometer 3‑axis time‑series (250 Hz), converted to STFT spectrogram images (per axis)
Training Data Size: Uses RéSiF/OMIV Super‑Sauze catalogue: totals — Rockfall 402, Earthquake 382, Micro‑quake 234, Noise 340; training+validation is first 70% chronologically of each class; testing last 30% per class
Input Shape: 65 × 66 × 3 (STFT image width × height × channels; 3 axes)
Label Source: RéSiF/OMIV catalogue (Provost et al.): STA/LTA frequency‑domain detector + Random Forest + expert validation; includes some uncertain labels

Title: A semblance-based microseismic event detector for DAS data
Category: Seismology · Microseismic monitoring · Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS)
Application: Detection of microseismic events during Enhanced Geothermal System (EGS) stimulation at Utah FORGE
Country: Study site: USA (Utah FORGE); Authors’ affiliations: Italy (Univ. of Pisa), Switzerland (ETH Zürich/SED), Germany (Ruhr Univ. Bochum)
University/Institute: Univ. of Pisa; Univ. of Geneva; DESTeC Univ. of Pisa; Ruhr Univ. Bochum; Swiss Seismological Service (ETH Zürich)
Purpose / Objective: Introduce and validate HECTOR, a semblance‑based detector that scans waveform coherence along hyperbolic trajectories to detect DAS microseismic events without a velocity model
Data Type: DAS strain/strain‑rate array data (borehole, downhole fibre)
Training Data Size: Not applicable (algorithmic method, no ML training)
Input Shape: Real data saved in 15 s files; fibre length 2482 m; channel spacing 1 m; gauge length 10 m; acquisition at 4 kHz; processing downsampled to 600 Hz
Number of Classes / Labels: Not applicable (event/no‑event thresholding, no labeled classes)
Event Types: Induced microseismic events (P and S arrivals), short inter‑event spacing
Label Source: Reference comparison: Silixa DAS microseismic catalogue for the same 24 h interval

Title: An Active Learning Framework for Microseismic Event Detection
Category: Microseismic monitoring • Active learning • Deep learning
Application: Detecting induced microseismic events during geothermal stimulation (Utah FORGE)
University/Institute: University of Strathclyde (Electronic & Electrical Engineering), Swiss Seismological Service – ETH Zürich
Purpose / Objective: Propose and test an active learning framework that selects the most informative samples to reduce labeling effort while maintaining/improving microseismic event detection performance; evaluate transfer across wells/sensor types
Data Type: Multichannel seismic time‑series (3‑component geophones), continuous recordings; STFT spectrograms derived from vertical component
Training Data Size: Per sensor: 24,779 windows used for training/query pool; 2,253 windows for testing
Input Shape: Time‑series: (3, 1600 samples = 400 ms). Spectral: 2 channels × 65 freq bins × 161 time steps (real & imag)
Number of Classes / Labels: Binary (event mask positive between P and S arrivals; negative elsewhere)
Event Types: Induced microseismic events (pulse‑like, high‑frequency, low SNR)
Label Source: EQ‑Transformer auto picks (P/S arrivals) subsequently validated by seismologists via visual inspection

Title: An Autoencoder-Based Deep Learning Model for Enhancing Noise Characterization and Microseismic Event Detection in Underground Longwall Coal Mines Using Distributed Acoustic Sensing Monitoring
Category: Mining seismology; anomaly detection; distributed acoustic sensing (DAS)
Application: Microseismic event detection and machinery noise characterization in underground longwall coal mines
Type of Paper: Conference paper (58th US Rock Mechanics/Geomechanics Symposium, ARMA 24–0207)
University/Institute: Colorado School of Mines; Virginia Tech; NIOSH Spokane
Purpose / Objective: Develop an unsupervised, autoencoder-based workflow on DAS PSD images to detect seismic events and characterize noise; compare with surface-network catalog
Data Type: DAS strain-rate time series converted to Power Spectral Density (PSD) images; surface seismometer event catalog for comparison
Training Data Size: 960 PSD images of background noise (normal) selected from 1000 candidate 2‑s windows; 768 train (80%), 192 test (20%)
Input Shape: 512 × 512 RGB images; processed in batches of 64
Event Types: Microseismic events near longwall face (P/S arrivals; example event M∼1.2 shown)
Label Source: Surface seismic network catalog (for screening/verification) + visual inspection of PSD/time series

Title: Ensemble Learning Improves the Efficiency of Microseismic Signal Classification in Landslide Seismic Monitoring
Category: Landslide monitoring · Seismic signal classification · Machine learning
Application: Automatic multi-class classification of microseismic events for landslide early-warning/monitoring (Jiuxianping deep-seated landslide, Chongqing, China).
University/Institute: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology; Institute of Mountain Hazards and Environment, Chinese Academy of Sciences; University of Chinese Academy of Sciences; Xinjiang University; Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Prevention and Control in Mining and Metallurgy.
Purpose / Objective: Build and compare classical ML vs. ensemble ML models for classifying landslide microseismic signals; assess accuracy/efficiency; apply best model to continuous monitoring series.
Data Type: Microseismic waveform records (3 components per station) detected from continuous field monitoring; converted to 60 handcrafted features (time, frequency, time–frequency, multi-station).
Training Data Size: 4,131 labeled events (Jan 2021–Apr 2022). Train/test per class given: HF 219/68; HFS 176/56; HLF 376/84; HLFS 86/23; LF 1082/298; LFS 177/35; Noise 1175/260; Rockfall 13/3.
Input Shape: Tabular feature vectors of length 60 per event (not raw waveforms).
Event Types: HF, HFS, HLF, HLFS, LF, LFS, Noise (N), Rockfall.
Label Source: Manual labeling using explicit duration/frequency-based criteria (Appendix A2).

Title: Deep learning‐based 3D microseismic event direct location using simultaneous surface and borehole data
Category: Microseismic monitoring; Seismology; Geophysics + Machine Learning
Application: Direct 3D location of microseismic events using joint surface + borehole recordings; validated on Utah FORGE field data and synthetic data
Type of Paper: Research Article (Journal of Geophysical Research: Machine Learning and Computation)
Country: Authors: Saudi Arabia (KAUST, Jeddah); Field site: USA (Utah FORGE)
University/Institute: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST), Physical Science and Engineering Division
Purpose / Objective: Propose and validate a deep learning method (modified DETR) to directly detect and 3D‑locate microseismic events by leveraging simultaneous surface and borehole data; demonstrate improved accuracy/reliability and domain adaptation from synthetic→field
Data Type: Passive seismic waveforms. Surface: two rings of 3‑component geophones; Borehole: string of 12 three‑component accelerometers. Synthetic data (elastic modeling) + field data (Utah FORGE). For field application, S‑wave horizontal component emphasized.
Training Data Size: Synthetic experiments: 500 event segments + 100 pure‑noise segments. Field‑application model: 1,000 synthetic inputs (surface & borehole) for supervised training.
Input Shape: Segments: simulated 1.6 s; processed via cross‑correlation & segmentation to 0.8 s (time‑lag). Surface array subset: two concentric rings (≈30 sensors each); Borehole: 12 levels at 30.5 m spacing. Components 3C (synthetic) / horizontal S‑wave for field runs.
Number of Classes / Labels: Binary detection (event / no event) + continuous regression of source location (x, y, z).
Event Types: Induced microseismicity during hydraulic stimulation (Utah FORGE).
Label Source: Synthetic forward modeling with known source coordinates (velocity model from tomography; wavelets estimated from field), used as supervision for training.

Title: DASEventNet: AI‑based microseismic detection on distributed acoustic sensing data from the Utah FORGE well 16A (78)‑32 hydraulic stimulation
Category: Microseismic detection • Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) • Geophysics
Application: Detecting microearthquakes (MEQs) during three‑stage hydraulic stimulation at Utah FORGE to build a more complete event catalog and characterize fracture geometry
Type of Paper: Research Article (Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth)
Country: USA (study site: Utah, USA). Collaborating institutions also in Italy.
University/Institute: Pennsylvania State University (Department of Geosciences; EMS Energy Institute; School of EECS); La Sapienza Università di Roma
Purpose / Objective: Develop and validate a deep learning detector (DASEventNet, ResNet‑50) for DAS microseismic event vs noise classification; apply to 7 days of continuous DAS data; compare against STA/LTA and prior catalogs; analyze spatiotemporal seismic response
Data Type: Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) strain/strain‑rate data (Silixa interrogator)
Training Data Size: 2,584 labeled examples: 1,292 MEQs + 1,292 noise (balanced)
Input Shape: 2‑second 2D windows: 1,021 channels × 2,000 samples (after decimation to 1 kHz)
Label Source: MEQ candidates from Silixa STA/LTA catalog; rigorous manual checks; noise windows chosen from non‑stimulation periods

Title: Enhancing microseismic event detection with TransUNet: A deep learning approach for simultaneous pickings of P‑wave and S‑wave first arrivals
Category: Seismic phase picking / Microseismic event detection (Deep Learning)
Application: Microseismic monitoring for gas storage reservoirs and coal seam roof fracturing; hydraulic fracturing/reservoir monitoring
University/Institute: School of Earth Sciences & Engineering, Xi’an Shiyou University; Key Lab of Intelligent Geophysical Exploration (Shaanxi HEI); Research Institute of Petroleum Exploration & Development (Beijing); E&D Research Institute of PetroChina Liaohe Oilfield (Panjin)
Purpose / Objective: Propose and validate a TransUNet-based method to simultaneously pick first arrivals of P and S waves from microseismic waveforms with high accuracy and robustness under noise; compare against STA/LTA, UNet, and SwinUNet
Data Type: Synthetic microseismic waveforms and field microseismic records; converted to 512×512 RGB images (R=G=B) with label images (P=1, S=−1, background=0)
Training Data Size: Synthetic: 100 simulated events × 50 geophones; Field: gas storage 52 events × 27 stations; coal seam 650 events × 33 stations; total ≈70,754 waveform channels used across train/test
Input Shape: 512 × 512 (RGB image per multi-channel event)
Event Types: Microseismic events (P and S first arrivals)
Label Source: Synthetic: theoretical labels from simulation; Field: manual picks/labels from experts

Title: ZSN2N‑DAS: Zero‑Shot Noise2Noise Denoising for Distributed Acoustic Sensing Data
Category: Seismology • Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) • Denoising • Self‑supervised Learning
Application: Denoising microseismic DAS recordings (glacier/ice stream data); improving arrival‑time picking and downstream seismology tasks
Type of Paper: Journal article (IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing, Vol. 63)
University/Institute: Xi’an Jiaotong University (School of Mathematics & Statistics; School of Information & Communications Engineering)
Purpose / Objective: Propose a zero‑shot Noise2Noise (N2N) self‑supervised denoising method for DAS that suppresses incoherent (zero‑mean) noise, mitigates signal leakage, improves SNR and phase‑picking quality versus N2N‑DAS
Data Type: DAS strain‑rate data (field, Rutford Ice Stream, Antarctica) and synthetic DAS microseismic dataset
Training Data Size: Zero‑shot on a single noisy DAS dataset (no clean labels). Synthetic set contains 5 microseismic events (1 km fiber, 1 m spacing, 1 s duration at 1000 Hz)
Input Shape: 2‑D DAS frames (time × channel); downsampled pairs y₁,y₂ created by stride‑2 convolutions
Label Source: Self‑supervised: noisy‑to‑noisy targets formed via fixed convolutional downsampling (k₁,k₂)

Title: An Intelligent First‑Arrival Picking Method of Microseismic Signals Based on the Small Sample Expansion
Category: Seismic signal processing; Microseismic first‑arrival picking; Deep learning
Application: Automatic P‑wave first‑arrival picking under low SNR and small‑sample conditions
Type of Paper: Journal article (IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing)
University/Institute: China Three Gorges University (Hubei Key Lab of Intelligent Vision‑Based Monitoring); East China Normal University; Yangtze University; China University of Geosciences (Beijing)
Purpose / Objective: Propose G‑LA‑MSG (GAN‑based small‑sample expansion) + AOG‑PSPNet (deep 1‑D PSPNet with AOG blocks) to improve first‑arrival picking accuracy/robustness at low SNR
Data Type: Synthetic microseismic records + real field microseismic records (ground and downhole)
Training Data Size: Base set: 10,000 synthetic + 20,000 real (total 30,000). Split 6:2:2 → 18,000 train / 6,000 val / 6,000 test. Additional 10,000 generated samples used to expand training of AOG‑PSPNet (≈28,000 training traces).
Input Shape: 1‑D waveform traces; examples include records length 4000–6000 ms at 1 ms sampling interval (single‑component traces).
Number of Classes / Labels: 2 (first‑arrival vs non‑first‑arrival at each time sample)
Event Types: Microseismic P‑wave first arrivals (synthetic + real; low to high SNR)
Label Source: Manually labeled first‑arrival times for training/validation/testing

Title: A Semi‑Supervised Approach to Characterise Microseismic Landslide Events from Big Noisy Data
Category: Semi‑supervised learning; Seismic signal classification; Landslides
Application: Characterising and labelling microseismic landslide events (earthquakes, micro‑quakes, rockfalls, anthropogenic noise) from continuous noisy data
University/Institute: University of Strathclyde, Glasgow, UK; data from OMIV/RESIF (Super‑Sauze, French Alps)
Purpose / Objective: Propose a semi‑supervised Siamese network that learns class‑exemplar anchors to auto‑label seismic signals with soft‑labelling + thresholding, reducing manual labelling effort and explaining outputs; classify four landslide‑related classes
Data Type: Three‑axis seismometer time‑series converted to STFT spectrogram images
Training Data Size: Used fewer labelled events than prior supervised baselines; training examples per class (approx.): Rockfall 126, Earthquake 104, Micro‑quake 131, Noise 190
Input Shape: 65 × 66 × 3 (width × height × channels) STFT image per 10 s window
Label Source: Résif catalogue: STA/LTA detection in frequency domain → supervised Random Forest → expert validation

Title: Multi-Branch Hybrid Convolution–Transformer for Enhanced Microseismic Signal Classification
Category: Microseismic signal classification; mining safety; deep learning
Application: Automatic classification of mine microseismic, blasting, and noise signals; real-time edge monitoring
Country: Malaysia; Indonesia (authors). Datasets: China (Dongguashan, Tongkuangyu); USA (Utah FORGE, cross-domain)
University/Institute: Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS (Malaysia); Universitas Islam Indonesia (Indonesia)
Purpose / Objective: Propose MBHCT (hybrid 1D/2D CNN + Transformer with learnable gate) to improve microseismic/blast/noise classification accuracy and enable real-time deployment
Data Type: Single-channel seismic waveforms, 1 s duration, 10 kHz sampling, 24-bit (primary dataset)
Training Data Size: Dongguashan: 2,400 traces total (800 per class) with stratified splits (Train 1,440 / Val 480 / Test 480). Cross-domain Utah FORGE subset for robustness (see results).
Input Shape: 16 × 16 feature matrix per trace (16 overlapping frames × 16 time–frequency descriptors)
Label Source: In-situ labelling by trained technicians; cross-checked in control room

Title: DeFault: DEep‑Learning‑Based FAULT Delineation Using the IBDP Passive Seismic Data at the Decatur CO₂ Storage Site
Category: Geophysics · Passive Seismic · Fault/Fracture Delineation · CCUS Monitoring
Application: Relocate passive microseismic events and delineate faults/fractures to support safe CO₂ storage at the Illinois Basin–Decatur Project (IBDP), Decatur, Illinois, USA.
Country: Study site: United States (Illinois). Author affiliations: USA & Saudi Arabia.
University/Institute: Los Alamos National Laboratory; Google Research; King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST); University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill; Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory.
Purpose / Objective: Propose and validate DeFault, a deep‑learning workflow with domain adaptation (MLReal) to (i) relocate passive seismic sources from borehole data, (ii) automatically cluster events, and (iii) fit/delineate fault planes efficiently and accurately for CCUS monitoring.
Data Type: Field: passive seismic monitoring records from IBDP (Z‑component), 31 downhole sensors (29 in GM‑1 well + 2 in CCS‑1 well). Synthetic: 3‑D acoustic FD simulations using 2011 stacking/interval velocity model.
Training Data Size: 15,000 synthetic samples (event locations as labels); split 12,000 train / 3,000 validation. Field data used for inference/testing (unlabeled).
Input Shape: 4‑second detection windows per event; 31 traces (Z‑component) arranged by receiver depth; sampling not specified. Network outputs a 3‑D heat‑map grid of [158 × 198 × 55] (X, Y, Z).
Number of Classes / Labels: Regression to 3‑D source location (no discrete classes). Labels for training from synthetic event coordinates.
Event Types: Microseismic events associated with CO₂ injection (passive monitoring).
Label Source: Synthetic simulations (event locations converted to Gaussian heat‑maps). Field catalog (Dando et al., 2021) used for comparison only.

Title: CAE Neural Network and Reverse‑Time Localization Imaging Method for Picking of Downhole Fracturing Microseismic Signal
Application: Picking microseismic waveforms in downhole/coal‑seam environments and localizing sources for hydraulic fracturing monitoring
University/Institute: Shanxi Geophysical and Geochemical Prospecting Institute; College of Mining Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology
Purpose / Objective: Integrate Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT), Convolutional Autoencoder (CAE), and K‑means to robustly pick microseismic waveforms under low SNR; then perform reverse‑time localization imaging (maximum‑amplitude and cross‑correlation) to accurately locate sources.
Data Type: (1) Synthetic/simulated microseismic data (surface shot and layered velocity model); (2) Real coal‑mine transmission channel wave data (KDZ‑3114 seismograph).
Training Data Size: Not explicitly reported (CAE used in an unsupervised/autoencoding fashion on time‑frequency images).
Input Shape: Time–frequency scalograms of size Nt × 256 (scale dimension = 256); Ntrace instances.
Event Types: Microseismic events (including channel‑wave events in coal seam); three synthetic sources in layered model.
Label Source: Unsupervised K‑means clustering (k=2); waveform parts labeled 1, others 0.

Title: Unsupervised Clustering of Microseismic Events and Focal Mechanism Analysis at the CO2 Injection Site in Decatur, Illinois
Category: Seismology · Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) · Machine Learning
Application: Microseismic event clustering and focal mechanism analysis to characterize small‑scale faults during CO₂ injection at IBDP (Decatur, Illinois)
University/Institute: Sandia National Laboratories; Colorado School of Mines; Massachusetts Institute of Technology; Illinois State Geological Survey
Purpose / Objective: Improve fault characterization and event classification at IBDP by combining spectral, unsupervised ML (NMF→HMM→K‑means) with focal mechanism (HASH) analysis; identify distinct microseismic patterns, slip mechanisms, and temporal bursts linked to injection.
Data Type: Downhole microseismic waveforms (vertical + horizontal components) from deep‑well geophones (PS3‑1, PS3‑2) and GM1 array; event catalogs from IBDP.
Training Data Size: Focused analysis on first‑year high‑seismicity region: MS Cluster #2 (698 events) and MS Cluster #4 (745 events); focal‑mechanism bursts retained 103/147 events (MS#2) and 63/114 (MS#4) after QC.
Input Shape: STFT spectrograms from PS3‑1 three‑component waveforms; sampling Δt = 0.0005 s (≈2 kHz); STFT window length 144 samples, overlap 132 samples; bandpass 10–400 Hz.
Number of Classes / Labels: Unsupervised K‑means with K = 2 clusters selected (per PCA separation & silhouette scores).
Event Types: Induced microseismic events during CO₂ injection (basement faults beneath Mt. Simon).
Label Source: Unsupervised clustering (no manual labels).

Title: Downhole Microseismic Detection Using Fiber-Optic Distributed Acoustic Sensing Based on Segmentation Model and Connected Domain Algorithm
Category: Microseismic detection; Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS); Oil & Gas / Geothermal
Application: Downhole DAS microseismic event detection (Utah FORGE EGS dataset)
University/Institute: Chengdu University of Technology (State Key Lab of Geohazard Prevention & Geoenvironment Protection; Key Laboratory of Earth Exploration & Information Technology)
Purpose / Objective: Propose SegDetection (semantic segmentation with dynamic snake convolution + MobileNetV3 + LRASPP) and connected-domain postprocessing to improve DAS downhole microseismic detection accuracy and speed.
Data Type: Fiber‑optic DAS microseismic data (2000 Hz sampling; ~1 km downhole well)
Training Data Size: 77 selected files (~11 GB) → 7,480 slices after augmentation (6,480 event slices; 1,000 pure-noise slices)
Input Shape: 896 × 992 per slice
Number of Classes / Labels: Binary segmentation: microseismic vs background (converted to detection via connected components)
Label Source: Manually annotated/segmented on visually discernible events (selected files)

Title: Joint Microseismic Event Detection and Location with a Detection Transformer
Application: Joint detection and source location of microseismic events (surface passive seismic); demonstrated on SEAM Time Lapse synthetic and Arkoma Basin field data
University/Institute: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST)
Purpose / Objective: Unify microseismic event detection (classification) and source location (regression) into a single end‑to‑end framework for real‑time monitoring
Data Type: Synthetic passive seismic (acoustic, P‑wave) and real field passive seismic (vertical geophones)
Training Data Size: SEAM example: 500 zero‑event, 2000 one‑event, 2000 two‑event, 2000 three‑event segments (synthetic); Arkoma example training: 2000 one‑event + 500 zero‑event (synthetic)
Input Shape: Array recordings treated as 2D images (receivers × time‑lag window). SEAM: 141 surface receivers at 30 m spacing; Arkoma field line used 56 traces (subset of 122) after preprocessing
Number of Classes / Labels: For training/evaluation: number of events per segment ∈ {0,1,2,3}; per‑slot event vs “no‑event” for DETR classification branch
Label Source: Synthetic simulations provide ground‑truth source coordinates and event counts; field “reference locations” taken from prior analyses for comparison

Title: PhysLoc‑DeepONet: Physics‑Constrained DeepONet for Passive Seismic Source Localization With Limited Differential Travel‑Time Data
Category: Geophysics · Machine Learning · Neural Operator / PINN‑constrained regression
Application: Passive/microseismic source localization for hydraulic fracturing, induced seismicity monitoring, mineral exploration
University/Institute: Yangtze University (Key Laboratory of Exploration Technologies for Oil & Gas Resources, MoE); Zhejiang University (School of Earth Sciences); The University of Texas at Austin (Bureau of Economic Geology)
Purpose / Objective: Propose a physics‑constrained DeepONet (PhysLoc‑DeepONet) that predicts travel‑time fields from limited differential travel‑time inputs and localizes sources by finding the field minimum; maintain high accuracy under sparse/low‑quality data
Data Type: Synthetic (forward‑modeled) differential travel‑times + Field case (ToC2ME) first‑arrival picks; includes spatial grid coordinates and velocity field values
Training Data Size: Per experiment: ~1,000 simulated sources; for each, ~100,000 randomly sampled grid points (coordinates + velocity + travel‑time). Additional noisy (≤5% Gaussian) training for ToC2ME; ablation on 100–1,000 training sources
Input Shape: Branch input: vector of differential travel‑times length = #stations (e.g., 65/49); Trunk input: 3‑D spatial coordinate (x, y, z) per grid point
Label Source: Synthetic labels from eikonal‑based forward modeling (Pyekfmm/FMM); field “reference” locations from an established workflow (ToC2ME)
